I'm a fan of making DB limits 10x-100x larger than is reasonable. Your estimates for what's reasonable will be too low around 1% of the time so when there are hundreds of fields in the database, you'll end up with doing the useless work of raising these limits multiple times.
The only use of these limits is just to protect against operational issues arising from too much garbage in the DB, but that's a threat only if it's like a million times the expected amount of data.

@CryptoGangsta @r2cdev Heya, I worked on this feature! In case you're curious, what we do is actually a bit more nuanced. We first scan all modified files. Then we scan the old versions of files that had issues, and dispose of any issues that appear there as well.

@CryptoGangsta @r2cdev We find out if an issue is the same by looking at a tuple of (rule ID, file path, line of code minus indentation). This way you don't get false positives if you're just refactoring a file, moving blocks of code around.
